race.name: Haute Route Ventoux
race.tagline: 3 stages, 3 summits of Mont Ventoux, 270 kilometers, and 7,500 meters of climbing through Provence. Every stage finishes atop the Giant of Provence — from a different side each day.

vitals.date: Historically early October (check hauteroute.org for 2026 status)
vitals.date_specific: 2026: DISCONTINUED. Haute Route collapsed to Alps-only since 2025 (now run by French Velo Evenements/FVE). Ventoux edition last ran in 2021.
vitals.registration: Via hauteroute.org when event is scheduled. Historically part of the Haute Route Series. Entry includes full logistics, feed stations, timing, mechanical support, and post-stage meals. Haute Route now focuses on the Alps event; Ventoux may return as a standalone 3-day format.
vitals.entry_fee: EUR 600-900 (varies by registration phase; includes logistics, feed stations, timing, post-stage meals)
vitals.feed_zones: Multiple feed stations per stage with water, energy food, and mechanical support. Post-stage hot meal included. Massage and recovery services at finish village.
vitals.cutoff_time: Daily cut-off times enforced — typically 8-10 hours per stage depending on distance
route_options: Compact Course: ~215 km / 5,300m across 3 stages (shorter daily options)

climate.primary: Provencal autumn — warm valleys, extreme exposure on Ventoux summit
climate.description: October in Provence brings average highs of 18-22C (64-72F) in the valleys around Bedoin with pleasant riding conditions on lower roads. Mont Ventoux's summit at 1,912m is a radically different climate zone: temperatures regularly drop 15-20C from the base, winds exceed 90 km/h on exposed days, and the barren moonscape above Chalet Reynard (km 15 of the Bedoin ascent) offers zero shelter. The mountain creates its own weather — clear skies at the base can coexist with cloud, rain, or even snow at the summit. The Mistral wind is the signature threat: this cold, dry northwesterly can gust to 100+ km/h and makes the exposed final 6 km genuinely dangerous.
challenges: Mont Ventoux summit winds — the Mistral can exceed 90 km/h with gusts to 100+ km/h above treeline
15-20C temperature differential between Bedoin (300m) and Ventoux summit (1,912m)
Three consecutive days of summit finishes compound fatigue and exposure
Exposed moonscape above Chalet Reynard — no trees, no shelter for the final 6 km
October rain at altitude can produce near-freezing conditions on summit

terrain.primary: Paved mountain roads with three ascents of Mont Ventoux
terrain.surface: Entirely paved on well-maintained French departmental and national roads. The three routes up Mont Ventoux — Bedoin (south), Malaucene (north), and Sault (east) — are classic Tour de France roads with excellent surface quality. Valley roads through Provence feature rolling terrain through vineyards and orchards. The Gorges de la Nesque provides dramatic canyon riding. No gravel, no technical surfaces — the challenge is purely gradient, distance, and altitude combined with Ventoux's savage exposure.
features: Bedoin ascent: 21.5 km at 7.6% average, summit at 1,912m — the classic Tour de France route, steepest sustained climbing
Malaucene ascent: 21.0 km at 7.5% average — the north face, early steep gradients with brief respite mid-climb
Sault ascent: 26 km at 4.4% average — the gentlest approach through lavender fields, longest route to summit
Exposed moonscape above treeline: final 6 km of each route crosses barren limestone with zero wind shelter
Gorges de la Nesque: dramatic canyon road with 400m cliffs — featured on approach stages
Rolling Provencal wine country between stage segments

course_description.character: A 3-day stage race centered entirely on Mont Ventoux — cycling's most legendary standalone mountain. Each stage radiates from Bedoin through the surrounding Provencal countryside before converging on the summit at 1,912m via a different ascent route. The course design is elegant in its simplicity: three days, three sides, one mountain. The climbing is relentless, the exposure above treeline is savage, and the cumulative fatigue of three consecutive summit finishes separates those who trained from those who hoped.
course_description.signature_challenge: The cumulative summit assault: three ascents of Mont Ventoux (1,912m) in three days from three different sides. Each approach has distinct character, but all converge on the same exposed, wind-battered final 6 km of moonscape. By Stage 3, the climb you know is waiting breaks you mentally before the gradient breaks you physically.

logistics.airport: Marseille Provence (MRS) 120 km south (~1.5 hr drive). Lyon Saint-Exupery (LYS) 240 km north (~2.5 hr drive). Avignon TGV station 60 km southwest (~1 hr drive). Rental car recommended from any arrival point.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Bedoin has limited accommodation — small hotels and gites fill rapidly for the event. Malaucene (15 km north) and Carpentras (20 km south) offer more options. Vaison-la-Romaine (25 km north) is a charming base with good hotel selection. Haute Route offers accommodation packages at additional cost. Book early — Provencal tourism is strong even in October.
logistics.food: Provence delivers exceptional cuisine. Bedoin has several restaurants and cafes. The regional specialties — ratatouille, tapenade, Provencal herbs, local wines from Cotes du Ventoux and Cotes du Rhone — are world-class. Haute Route provides post-stage hot meals. The ristori on course are well-stocked. After three summit finishes, the evening meals in Provencal restaurants are deeply satisfying.

history.origin_story: The Haute Route Ventoux was created as a shorter-format addition to the Haute Route Series, which began with the flagship 7-day Alps event in 2011. The concept was compelling: take cycling's most legendary standalone mountain and create a 3-day stage race that summits it from every side. Based in the village of Bedoin at the southern foot of Ventoux, the event leveraged the Haute Route brand's professional-level organization and applied it to a focused, geographically concentrated challenge. The 3-day format made the event accessible to riders who could not commit to the week-long Alps or Dolomites events. Mont Ventoux's unique status — featured in the Tour de France 15+ times since 1951, site of Tom Simpson's death in 1967, and crowned by an iconic observatory on its barren summit — made it the perfect location for an event that transcends cycling into pilgrimage.
notable_moments: 2018: Inaugural Haute Route Ventoux — 3-day format established with daily summit finishes
2019: Event grows to attract international field of 400+ riders from 20+ nationalities
2021: Post-COVID return — October edition with memorable summit conditions including near-freezing temperatures
Haute Route Series evolves: Alps remains flagship, shorter-format events (including Ventoux) adjusted based on demand
Mont Ventoux continues to feature in Tour de France, including memorable double-ascent stage in 2021
history.reputation: Recognized as the definitive Ventoux multi-day challenge. The Haute Route brand brings credibility and professional-level support that distinguishes this from self-organized Ventoux rides. Cycling media consistently rate the Haute Route events as the gold standard for amateur multi-day racing.

biased_opinion.summary:
Mont Ventoux is cycling's most mythological mountain — a barren, windswept colossus that has broken professionals in the Tour de France and claimed the life of Tom Simpson in 1967. The Haute Route Ventoux takes this legendary climb and demands you do it three times in three days, each from a different side. 270 km. 7,500 meters of climbing. Three summit finishes at 1,912m on the exposed moonscape where the Mistral can gust past 90 km/h.

The Bedoin ascent is the classic: 21.5 km at 7.6% average, with a murderous 10 km stretch through the forest averaging 9-10%. The Malaucene side hits you immediately with 12% gradients before a brief mid-climb reprieve. The Sault approach lulls you with 26 km of gentle lavender-country climbing before the exposed final kilometers deliver the same savage summit as the other two sides. By Stage 3, your legs have climbed to 1,912m twice already. The third ascent is where character is revealed.

The Haute Route organization is world-class — feed stations, mechanical support, timing, post-stage meals, and daily rankings make this feel like a professional stage race for amateur riders. The field of 300-500 creates genuine camaraderie forged through shared suffering. Provence as a backdrop — vineyards, gorges, medieval villages — adds culture and cuisine to complement the climbing. This is not a race for beginners. It is a pilgrimage for serious cyclists who understand that Ventoux is not just a mountain — it is a test of everything you are.
strengths: Three ascents of cycling's most legendary mountain — each from a different side, each with distinct character
7,500m of climbing in 3 days with professional Haute Route support and logistics
Provencal setting — wine country, gorges, medieval villages, and world-class cuisine
Intimate field of 300-500 riders creates genuine stage-race camaraderie
Professional-level organization: feed stations, Mavic mechanical support, timing, post-stage meals
Achievable in a long weekend — 3-day format versus 7-day Alps commitment
weaknesses: EUR 600-900 entry fee is steep for a 3-day event — excludes budget-conscious cyclists
Event status uncertain — Haute Route has consolidated around the Alps format, Ventoux may not run annually
Bedoin is remote — rental car required, limited accommodation in the village itself
Mistral wind on exposed summit can be genuinely dangerous — events have been modified for safety
Three consecutive summit finishes at 1,912m with cumulative fatigue is a serious physiological challenge
No distance options within the competitive format — you summit Ventoux every day or you do not finish
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If Mont Ventoux is on your bucket list, the Haute Route Ventoux is the definitive way to experience it. Three ascents, three sides, three days of world-class organization in Provence. The price is high and the climbing is brutal, but there is no other event that delivers this specific combination of legendary climbing, professional support, and Provencal culture. Confirm the event is running before booking — Haute Route's event calendar has evolved in recent years.

final_verdict.one_liner: Three summits of cycling's most legendary mountain in three days — the definitive Ventoux experience with professional stage-race support.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Ride this if Mont Ventoux is your cycling grail and you want all three ascents in a professionally supported format. Skip it if you are on a budget (EUR 600-900 entry), prefer flat or gravel terrain, or cannot commit to 3 consecutive days of serious climbing. If you just want to climb Ventoux once, ride it independently — it is a public road.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a single Ventoux ascent: ride independently from Bedoin, Malaucene, or Sault. For a longer Haute Route: Haute Route Alps (7 days, 800 km, 20,000m). For a similar 3-day alpine challenge: Haute Route Alps 3-day format. For a Ventoux-adjacent sportive: Cardiac Ventoux or Tour du ALS.

to less and then become less severe and
it was very motivating to not just see
the kilometers take by but to also see
the gradient remaining decrease it
becomes a lot more manageable in my head
I was just breaking things down and to
be in a position where I'm going fairly
hard here I'm riding very close to my
limit but I'm still able to have this
internal voice and rationalize and keep
thinking about okay how many people are
in front of me you know how far have we
got left to go what's the gradient for
this next kilometer what's coming up is
there any shade I can ride under all of
these things I was thinking about you
know if I can cut the corn ever so
slightly without being on the other side
of the road I'll do that you know all
these small little things I was just
thinking about all the time and I knew
that it was I was on a great day because
I was able to think about that and I
think about all these things at the same
time so the whole clim for me took 1
Hour 2 minutes 59 seconds this was at
328 wats normalized power or 5.4 watts
per kilog and 95% of my FTP the source
anadon for straa has an interesting
metric in that it can estimate sea level
power now obviously we're climbing to
2,000 M and it estimated my sea level
power to be around 10 watts higher than
my average if we compare that to a sea
level time TR I did a couple of weeks
ago where I averaged around about
355 Watts for 55 minutes safely say that
I performed fairly well with slight
fatigue and obviously altitude and heat
all being added stressers during this
event now to of France last came up here
in
2021 they did two ascents of this side
Tyler Pacha has the K actually from that
day of course and the speed they went up
there was incredible no doubt doing six
watts per kilogram or thereabouts to be
able to do that fresh is a big ask but
to be able to do that in a stage race or
back to back days at the end of a big
day as it was is incredible so I finally
reached Al reard and just as I'm getting
there I'm seeing one Rider in front of
me with a motorbike there were a couple
of people stood on the side of the road
and I I I asked them how many riders
were in front and I held up my hand I
was like is it three or four and someone
pointed at me and they said you are five
I am five so I saw this Rider in front
of me I thought right here's the first
Rider so as we came around the bend sh
rard I rode up to the back of this Rider
and we were starting now this long
process of going in and out of the
mountain side to reach the summit this
is the iconic section this is the bit
that you know dun on Swift and seen in
pictures seen at the Tor of France this
is the only section where you feel like
you're making progress and you can see
the top this section is also the section
that suits me the best because it's
slightly shallower
and I'm also coming from behind so I'm
extremely motivated to see the riders
that I'm catching one by one now at the
time I can only see this Rider in front
of me and some people around who have
also done the shorter route and they're
also just coming onto that section from
the other Ascent I ride up to the back
and going into one of the first
headwinds on this section I decide to
sit behind and see what the score is on
sitting behind I realized that this
Rider is starting to struggle i b my
time and I wait until we're just about
to turn into the tailwind and I ride
past now on riding past in a Tailwind
means that generally if a rid is tiring
you can get a slight gap on them because
the Tailwind is the only section where
mentally they're going to think about
getting a break so I make my move and I
get a small Gap we turn into the next
headwind and I'm making progress and now
we're approximately 5 to 6 km from the
summit for reference by the way the
Tommy Simpson Memorial is about 1.2 1.3
km from the top so now I'm in fourth
place is a Podium position up for grabs
you'd think so but I couldn't see any
riders in front of me not from my event
at least it was very difficult to
distinguish who was in my event and who
was in the shorter event now at this
moment in time I'm going past Riders and
I can start to see another Rider in
front that has another motorbike with
so I start to head towards that Rider I
get across that Rider and without
hesitation ride straight past this Rider
is obviously on their last legs and all
of these Riders have been in the
Breakaway for a considerable amount of
time I don't know how long exactly but
they've obviously been out there for a
while working together but they're now
splitting up and they're on their own so
I'm now in the third position we're
approaching about 3 2 and 1/2 km to go
and I start to see what can only be
described as a battle going on up front
there's two Riders riding side by side
with another motorbike now this
motorbike rider looks like to be a
proper camera motorbike as opposed to a
motorbike rider that's just a marshal or
you know police that was looking after
the other Riders so I assume that this
one is the front of the race but I could
be wrong I don't actually know at this
point so getting into this last 2 and
1/2 km I know we've got you know around
5 10 minutes of riding still to go we're
still in a lot of headwind and the last
section i' I was told prior to the event
that last bit is steep as and you have
to save something for it and if you got
a headwind it's even worse we did
actually have a headwind and eventually
I get to these two
Riders and one of them attacks the other
I'm within probably around 10 seconds 15
seconds at this point of bridging across
to them so I can see all this unfolding
there's a big attack he hits him on the
left hand side of the road and he he
sits down and he drives on now at this
point I'm thinking to myself this could
be the end like if this is the front of
the race I might not get across that rer
that's just attack like he's attacked at
a perfect time when I'm just coming
across to them because although he
probably doesn't know that I'm coming
from behind he does know that he's only
got 2 km left of the event so he's going
to go all in from here to the top now
like everything there's no point holding
back so if he's going all in and he
holds me off then there's a very real
chance that I'll come second I'll come
third now at the time remember like I
didn't even think I could Bridge across
to the breakway I didn't know where they
were I didn't know what the Gap was you
know at one point I was in a group of 30
that was maybe 2 and 1 half 3 minutes
behind so the way this is unfolding in
front of me is just incredible like I
almost can't believe it and it's filling
me with adrenaline it's filling me with
excitement just being there within a
shouting distance and it's bringing out
all of that
racing like nature and the the the thing
that that doesn't really come out a lot
with me it was coming out there because
it's been a long time since I've had
this opportunity presented itself like
this falling right in front of me and it
was like it was meant to happen because
of the way it happened so I make my way
up to the guy who's now in second place
and his head has dropped you can see
pedalin squares and puts up no fight
whatsoever when I ride past I see the
motorbike with the leader and the Gap is
not increasing if anything it's coming
back pretty quickly so I know that this
move that the Riders made to drop the
guy in second he obviously knows that
that's his move to potentially win the
event like he's not seen anybody else
come from behind maybe so I'm assuming
he thinks he's made this big effort he
now knows he's dropped that Rider and
he's just getting to the Finish now but
he's slowing down and whether or not
he's slowing down through tiredness I
don't know but at this point I'm just
chasing up to that back wheel and as I
get there a big bus comes up from the
left hand side you wouldn't believe it
it actually blocks some of the wind
that's coming off like really strongly
from the left hand side so I get a tiny
bit of shelter as I'm closing that gap
for the final time and I get up to the
back wheel and I do the same thing as I
did with that Rider back in fourth place
and I just sit there quietly
and the rider in front of me Julian
looks across sees the camera bike sees
somebody over his shoulder and then just
looks back there was only one thing I
was going to do and that was to go
straight through I wrote straight past
and I just roded the tempo that I had
been riding the whole time that 330 wat
sort of just
mundane TT effort that I knew had got me
across to all these riders in the first
place and on dropping Julian 30 seconds
later I had about 800 M left with the
climb to go and it almost felt and
looking back at the footage I can see
that where I got across to Julian it was
right as we passed Tommy Simpsons
Memorial but those last 800 M where I
was riding on my own I had to confirm
with the motorbike that I was in first
place and they had to confirm with me
that I was in 120 km event and not the
100 km event I turned around the final
bend the iconic final Bend drinking game
for the word
iconic come around and there's a finish
and I cross a Finish
Line and I don't know what to say it was
like the most it was truly the
most
unbelievable way to to ride
myself back into
contention
when really at that point the bottom of
the
climb it was a question of was this even
the right move or not I might official
time for the time segment of the climb
was in the 58 minutes 20 second range
which was nearly two plus minutes faster
than the next Rider so in hindsight if I
hadn't have made my move at the bottom
of the climb I would never have got
across and I certainly wouldn't have
caught you know the leader with such a
short amount of time remaining and
that's it that is the event in all its
entirety like I said it was one of those
days where things just went into place
and you just you couldn't make it up the
way it unfolded you just could not make
it up so special thanks to all my
sponsors supporters you guys for

Subscribe button please hit the like

next
one

A little about Ed….
Ed is an elite cyclist, 2019 British Hill Climb Champion and former Under 23 British RR Champion. He has raced as a Continental level Professional Rider for one of the most successful British cycling teams in Japan, Korea, Australia, Canada, Azerbaijan to name a few. He is also a public speaker, coach, and founded the on/offline cycling community, Backpedal. At the start of 2021, the Discord community began, and plonked many of Ed's viewers in to a 550+ strong group of people that had many things in common. Not least wanting to explore the world and pedal their bikes faster!

Ed coaches lots of cyclists in the online community too! If you are interested, email [laverackcoaching@yahoo.com] for information on the 1-2-1 coaching package and training plans, of which there are many and flexible options to suit everyone!

Ed now prioritises having fun with my hard earned fitness and knowledge in the sport. Ed is an elite cyclist, 2019 British Hill Climb Champion and former Under 23 British RR Champion. He has raced as a Continental level Professional Rider for one of the most successful British cycling teams in Japan, Korea, Australia, Canada, Azerbaijan to name a few. He is also a public speaker, coach, and founded the on/offline cycling community, Backpedal. At the start of 2021, the Discord community began, and plonked many of Ed's viewers in to a 550+ strong group of people that had many things in common. Not least wanting to explore the world and pedal their bikes faster!

Ed coaches lots of cyclists in the online community too! If you are interested, email [laverackcoaching@yahoo.com] for information on the 1-2-1 coaching package and training plans, of which there are many and flexible options to suit everyone!

Ed now prioritises having fun with my hard earned fitness and knowledge in the sport. Showcasing that maybe there is an alternative to racing for teams when you can race for yourself and your community of followers and brands. This YouTube channel is a docu-series highlighting what it’s like to be a cyclist in today’s competitive age of sport.

Returning to the Montons Grand Fondo, I
was ready to fight to retain my title
against a much stronger field than last
year. After an early crash, however, a
complicated feed station and nearly 25
km of chasing for the final 20 km climb
to the top of Vonto. Would I even have
any energy left to put up a fight? You
couldn't write this script. This is my
Vonu race video. Sit back, relax, like,
subscribe, and enjoy.
Now, in this year's edition of the
Mongon 2 Grand Fondo, I was in the 135
km race that took in two major climbs.
Now, of the three routes at this Grand
Fondo, I was doing the longest one, 135
km with 3,200 m of elevation gain,
finishing on top of Montbu, climbing the
Bedwan side, which is of course the most
famous side, the one that's linked to
the tour to France. Now, this route
actually only had two significant
climbs, the Calm and the Montu at the
end. Now the first climb came around mid
distance just over 13 km and an average
gradient of 5%. And do I even need to
tell you the mon 2 statistics tops out
at 1900 m and is around 20 km at 8%
average. Now there's a quick course
overview. Now equipment wise I was
riding my factor auto vam my lightweight
climbing bike. I was running my 28 32mm
black ink race wheels coupled with
Continental GP5000 TT tires setup with
Tubelito inner tube. Those tires
measured in at 28 mm. The gearing for
this was a
5236 with an 1130 cassette. Now, to
actually film this epic, I used the
Insta 360 Go 3S camera, the really tiny,
about 40 g action camera that was
mounted under my handlebars. Now, on
last year's video, I had 290 comments. I
want to try and beat that this year. And
I thought, leave a comment down below
that best describes using
emojis my emotions as I was claiming
Monu. Speaking of comments, a lot of you
said on last year's video that it was
really complicated and confusing that
nearly everybody on all different routes
was wearing the same event jersey and so
it was really hard to distinguish who
was in your race and who wasn't. That
didn't change this year. We were all
still in the same jerseys. However, I
was still wearing my Rule 28 Arrow socks
and my Rule 28 bib short. And of course,
I went for my Cask Valgro helmet for
extra breathability. So, let's get into
the race then. It all started with me
being on the front row. I was granted
that starting position because I was
last year's winner. So, I was able to
line up right in the front of the start
line in front of almost 5,000 other
riders taking on this event. Now, it's
actually pretty cool that I got to start
the front because I was with the the
lead car. So, it very much brought back
memories of me being in a real proper
bike race again. So, as you can imagine,
the first couple of kilometers are
fairly hectic. People are trying to get
from the back to the front, trying to
maintain position, and just generally
trying to be in a a safer position all
around to avoid any crashes. So, you
could actually feel the tension
building. Everybody's just trying to
jostle and find their feet in a
fastmoving pelon. The rods are as wide
as they are. Sometimes it makes it more
dangerous than when they're narrow.
There was this one fast straight
section, a descent. And I was sitting
around maybe 80th, 60th position. It's
really hard to tell when the bunch is
that deep, but I could definitely see
the front. And we hit this one section
of road where there was some kind of
traffic island or the road narrowed and
there was a big slam on the brakes in
front of me. And I had a little bit of
time to think about what I was going to
do, but I did actually start breaking
quite early, but because we were moving
at nearly 60 km an hour, I just did not
have a chance to stop. I was a little
bit late to the party, but I did join
the crash. Now, sadly, the YouTuber in
me is kicking myself that I wasn't
actually filming at this point. So, in
order to prove that I crashed, here's a
little clip of my little bloodied finger
on on the camera while I was riding
along cuz I thought, "No one's going to
believe me I crashed. I need to give
some kind of proof." I've been in far
worse crashes. Thankfully, the bike was
okay. I was just a little bit scraped
and bumped. One thing I learned being a

You give your bike a little once over,
you put your chain back on, you
straighten your levers, and then you

consuming a minimum of 500 milliliters

significant part of the race. riders at

thankfully found myself, were really
pushing the pace at the bottom of the

obvious in a sea of people wearing all

uh fresh bid on as we were going over
the top. We'd be doing a moving feed,
not hopefully me having to stop to grab
a bottle. Now, just a quick one. If
you're enjoying this video so far, then
please hit the like button and well,
subscribe if you're new here too. So,
the climb of the HMO we did in 33
minutes and 35 seconds. It's a 14 km
climb and our normalized power was just
under 300 W. Now, as you'd expect from a
climb this shallow, our van is around
about the,00 mark. Now, coming to the
top of the whole M, I positioned myself
to the back of the group. I could see
the feed station coming up at the top
and I was getting ready to get my
bottle. I had my other bottle in my
mouth ready to throw it and then I would
grab the other one and put it straight
into my empty bottle cage. Now, this
bottle would be quite important because
of the heat, but also because we'd have
approximately an hour down to the foot
of Mon 2, and I would need to consume
certainly at least 750 mls of liquid
from the top of here until there.
Because don't forget, you're always
fueling and hydrating for what's to
come. It's never about what's happening
right now. It's about what's happening
in 30 minutes, 60 minutes, 90 minutes
from now. So, as we got close to the
top, I could see my bottle was being
held out ready for me to grab. And as we
came up, you can't actually see it on a
video, but someone I think goes to grab
my bottle. Now, in this situation, I
can't comment whether or not someone
deliberately went to grab the bottle or
was trying to take a bottle from the
next person who was feeding on the side
of the road and accidentally swiped the
bottle and therefore knocked it onto the
floor. But all I know is my bottle was
now on the floor. And I had to actually
stop to pick it up and then get going
again. And in the moment, I was so
angry. I was so angry that this had
happened because it couldn't have
happened at a worse point. You see, from
the top of the Hott, we had 1 hour,
maybe just over an hour of quite fast
road. And not only was this water bottle
crucial, but I would also need to stay
with the group because this was the part
of the course where you're going to get
the most benefit from staying in a group
given that the speeds are so high. So by
stopping, I got dropped. But I hadn't
got any more water with me. I drank, I'd
fueled appropriately up until that
point, ready to receive another package
of nutrition so that I could keep going
and maintain my schedule. I
instinctively stopped. I didn't even
think about staying with the group and
risking it because I just thought this
is the game plan. And the problem with
going off the game plan in the heat and
with von 2 at the end, I might be
risking too much and I might not have
anything left for the mountain at the
end. So by stopping I found myself in a
small group and I stayed with them on
the descent and we could see for ages
the group in front of us and the group
still was a considerably large size. It
must have been at least 60 70 maybe even
80 riders. The Hallmmont climb, you
know, was long, you know, 30 minutes,
but it wasn't particularly hard. You
know, people still made it over the
climb. It was quite early into the event
and we didn't really race up it. And up
until then, a breakaway hadn't really
formed either because although the
course was slightly uphill, there wasn't
really any challenging moments. And with
now the majority of the course being
downhill to the bottom of two, I don't
think people were really thinking too
much about a breakaway at this point.
They were more thinking about conserving
until the mountain. However, while I was
chasing to get back on and slightly
frustrated by our progress because at
one point we were quite close and then
at another point we dropped and lost a
couple of seconds. A breakaway had
actually gone clear. Now, where it had
gone, I have no idea. I don't know where
they attacked, where they made a move.
I'm not sure. But by the time I got back
to the main pelaton, I could definitely
see once I got to the front that
potentially the the big names that I
knew were
there, including last year's runnerup,
was not in the group. And I knew for
sure they were off the front. Now, this
section of chasing for me and my small
group was around about 30 minutes at 240
watts normalized, which isn't a lot, but
I am highly certain that if I was sat in
the Pelaton, given how given our average
speed was close to 50 km an hour, I
think I could have been doing at least
50 watts less than what I was doing in
that small group. Now, we're about to
get to a pretty iconic part of the
course, the gorgeesque, which we
actually ascended last year, but we were
going down this year. It was quite
technical. There was a big drops either

group was happy to stay together and
just cruise down it. So, I wasn't going
to actually help the chase. I made that
decision pretty early on when I got back
to the group that I needed to prioritize

you know, the breakway had gone and I
wasn't in it, but I managed to ride the
breakaway back in and finish first.
However, this year I knew that with the
breakaway gone and potentially being
full of all of the favorites that I was
now in a position where I would have to
be on the back foot, but even more
significantly once we got to the bottom
of the mountain. Now, I wasn't going to
give chase because the terrain just
didn't lend itself to it. It was far too
fast to be actually eating into the
breakaways advantage. We've seen it in
professional races where if there's a
tailwind or if there's, you know, a
downhill run to the finish, it's very,
very hard to bring a breakaway back. And
when we eventually got to the bottom of
two, I did the same thing I did last
year. I attacked as soon as the road
went uphill. But I wanted to attack
early because I knew last year that if I
didn't, I would never have caught first
place or second place towards the top of
the climb. So, we had to try and eat
into the advantage as quickly as
possible. So, that's what I did. Now,
into the bottom of two, I picked up my
second feed of the day with AD and we
did a fantastically smooth bottle
handoff on the corner and that was it. I
was locked in. So, what's really hard to
summarize now is this final section of
the race, the actual mountain. Now, we
didn't by any means hit this climb
fresh. And we done about 2,500 kg of
work, which isn't a lot, but it's also
not nothing. Now, the pelon was still
quite big at the bottom of two. It was
probably around 50 60 riders. That
that's a lot more than it was last year.
The level of competition is certainly
higher this year at the event than it
was last year. There's more strength in
depth and that was obvious because of
the size of the group coming to the
bottom. So when I started the first
couple of kilometers of Mont 2, I was in
a similar position to I was last year. I
was around about eighth or ninth place
on the road. The breakaway was up the
road with approximately 2 minute of an
advantage. Now I didn't really know
this. I knew they were a fair way ahead
probably, but thankfully they did start
to split up as soon as they hit the
climb. So what happened over the course
of maybe the next 10 km until Shalley
Renard averages about 9 to 9 and a half%
for a long period of time. However,
after that the gradient tends to shallow
off slightly and you also get the wind
coming into play. So that last part from
Shal Renard to the top is usually my
strength. So, I got a couple of
kilometers into the climb, but I was
caught by one rider who just seemed to
teleport, to use the Lantern Rouge
language, across to me and then just
went straight past. Now, I had no chance
of getting on this rers's wheel and
there is really no draft. So, I let this
rider go and stayed and stayed at my own
pace. Then, about five riders came
across and joined me. They also just
went straight past, but three of the
five couldn't keep the pace of the two
that managed to just keep riding away
from us. So, we let those two go and
then the rest of us just kind of spread
out on the climb. We splintered into
individuals essentially. Now, as I said,
the leading group must have started to
fracture on the climb really early. Now,
the Bedan side has lots of straight
sections, and I was able to keep a lot
of the riders in front of me in my field
of view, and this really helped keep me
motivated. So, when we started to get
closer to Shai Renard, I noticed that I
not only was catching one or two riders,
but there was a cluster of riders, not
that far in front, about four in total.
Now, I managed to just put a little
surge in just before Shalley Renard to
get onto the back of this group to just
give myself a tiny bit of rest as we
went round the really, really long hair
pin at Shal Renard. And what I also
noticed was that there were a lot of
riders who were really backing off on
the shallower sections, but were holding
their own when the gradient got steeper
again. Now, I am somebody who can keep
my effort and power quite even even when
the gradient changes back and forth. And
this is where the charard section to the
top suits me down to the ground. So I
caught these four riders and I went
around them. I started a set pace
because I thought if I've caught them
and I'm doing about 320 W, then I should
keep going at that pace and hopefully be
on my own. But that was easier said than
done because the rider that finished
second last year was one of the riders
that was holding on to my wheel and it
took me a long time to shake off, but I
did push myself a little bit over the
limit to do so. Thankfully, I then had
enough of a gap to maintain all the way
to the finish. I didn't know what
position I'd come, of course, because we
were still catching riders who were on
the shorter course that had come up the
the sole side. So, I didn't know exactly
where I'd finished. However, afterwards,
I found out that I finished fifth, which
now I come to think of it, I'm pretty
proud of. Crashing, the heat,
bottlegate, my performance on the day
actually gives me a lot of confidence
because when I compare it to last
year's, it's very similar, but the
temperatures are much higher, maybe 10°
more. In fact, when I won it last year,
the conditions definitely suited me a
lot more. And I was able to do about 6
watts more for the 1h hour climb after
about 500 kg more than last year's event
as well. So, I was in a slightly more
fatigued state, able to produce slightly
more power for the 1-hour climb. Looking
at the data from M12, I did hit it quite
hard. The first five minutes at 376
watts normalized power before settling
in to hit around 324 W for just over an
hour, which is also just under 1,400 VA.
Fifth place in an arguably better, more
deeper competitive field. I'm pretty
happy with the way the race went, even
if I was a little bit banged up. If you
enjoyed this, hang around for the next
video, which is going to be a recon ride
of the attack to to France route coming
soon. So, make sure you subscribe. Like
I said, like the video, leave a comment
down below of the emojis, of the
emotions you thought I was going through
for this whole 1 hour scent in the heat,
and I will see you in that next video.
Thanks for watching.

videos.title: Mont Ventoux via Bédoin
videos.description:
Highlights of the climb from Bédoin to Mont Ventoux, filmed on Wed. April 12, 2017 by Alpine Cols.
For information on training camp, coaching and support during the Haute Route, go to https://alpinecols.com

quotes.text: All of that racing coming down to the final kilometers of one of the most iconic climbs in cycling history. We did one of the fastest descents I've ever recorded in a race and I was nearly out of the race completely before we'd even got to the bottom and the 20 km ascent to nearly 2,000 M of elevation to the finish.

After an early crash, a complicated feed station and nearly 25 km of chasing for the final 20 km climb to the top of Ventoux. Would I even have any energy left to put up a fight? You couldn't write this script.

key_challenges.name: Early crash section
key_challenges.description: Fast straight descent with traffic island or road narrowing that caused major crash at nearly 60 km/hour, catching many riders who couldn't brake in time.

key_challenges.name: The Colm climb
key_challenges.description: First significant climb at mid-distance, just over 13 km at 5% average gradient.

key_challenges.name: Final 20km climb to Mont Ventoux
key_challenges.description: 20 km ascent to nearly 2,000m elevation, 8% average gradient up the Bedoin side, which is the most famous side linked to the Tour de France.

key_challenges.name: Feed station complications
key_challenges.description: Complicated feed station led to nearly 25 km of chasing before the final climb.

terrain_notes.text: The Bedoin side is described as the most challenging of the three sides to climb Mont Ventoux, preferred by experienced riders over the shallower Soul side.
The Soul side offers a very gradual, shallow gradient compared to both the Malisan and Bedoin sides.
Roads can be dangerously narrow in sections, with traffic islands that cause sudden braking and crashes at high speeds.

gear_mentions.text: Ran 28mm Continental GP5000 TT tires with Tubelito inner tubes on 28-32mm wheels for the climbs.
Used 52/36 chainring with 11-30 cassette gearing for the mountain stages.

race_day_tips.text: Position yourself safely in first couple kilometers as people jostle from back to front - tension builds in the fast-moving peloton and crashes happen.
Be prepared for very hot conditions - temperatures expected to reach mid-30s, so finish the climb before it gets too hot.
Navigate feed stations carefully as complications can lead to long chasing efforts before the final climb.

additional_quotes.text: This Grand Fondo is one of the most legendary in the whole world - you can't get many finishes more iconic than having an event that finishes with a competitive nature like this at the top of Mont Ventoux itself.
It's packed today. Absolutely jam-packed with people. Cyclist, motorcyclist, cars, even a couple of lorries and buses. I've never seen it like that at the top. Cars parked everywhere.

rider_intel.search_text: The Haute Route Ventoux is described as one of the most legendary Grand Fondos worldwide, featuring multiple route options including 100km, 120km, and 135km distances with around 3,200m elevation gain. The signature challenge is the final 20km climb up Mont Ventoux's Bedoin side at 8% average gradient to nearly 2,000m elevation. Riders face early race dangers including crashes on fast descents with road narrowing at nearly 60km/hour. The course includes significant climbs like the Colm at mid-distance (13km at 5% gradient) and complicated feed stations that can force long chasing efforts. Conditions are expected to be very hot with temperatures reaching mid-30s. The Bedoin side is considered the most challenging of Ventoux's three climbing routes, preferred over the shallower Soul side. The event attracts nearly 5,000 riders and is extremely crowded with heavy vehicle traffic at the summit.
